title: Event-Driven Architecture
description: Architecture document for event-driven systems covering event design principles, Kafka-based event backbone, processing patterns (notification, state transfer, CQRS, event sourcing, saga), consistency guarantees, dead letter queues, and schema registry management.
anti_patterns: using events as commands (e.g., 'complete.trade' instead of 'trade.completed')
events that require callbacks to the source service for processing
modifying events after publication instead of publishing compensating events
breaking schema changes without versioning or new event types
ignoring event ordering within partition keys
no dead letter queue for failed event processing
non-idempotent event consumers that break on replay
embedding sensitive data in event payloads
no schema registry allowing incompatible event format changes
implementation_steps: Establish event design principles: events are facts not commands (e.g., 'trade.completed' not 'complete.trade'), self-contained (carry all data needed without callbacks), immutable (publish compensating events for corrections), versioned (backward-compatible schema evolution), and timestamped (both producer and processing timestamps)

Set up the Kafka event backbone: 5-broker cluster with 3-node ZooKeeper, NVMe storage per broker; organize topics by domain and entity using pattern '<domain>.<entity>.<event-type>' (e.g., 'inventory.item.acquired')

Configure topic partitioning: default 6 partitions for standard topics, 24 for high-throughput analytics topics; partition key typically userId to guarantee ordering per user; replication factor of 3 with min ISR of 2
Set retention policies: 7 days for standard topics, 30 days for audit topics, indefinite for event-sourced aggregates

Implement the Event Notification pattern: service publishes event to notify other services; consumers react independently with no expectation of response (e.g., notification service consumes trade.completed for push notifications)

Implement Event-Carried State Transfer: when a consumer needs data from another service but cannot call its API synchronously, include relevant state in the event; consumer maintains local materialized view (e.g., trading service includes item metadata in trade.completed events)

Implement CQRS: separate command side (processes writes, validates business rules, persists changes, emits events) from query side (consumes events, builds optimized read models, serves queries from materialized views with low latency); command side is source of truth, query side is eventually consistent

Implement Event Sourcing: store full sequence of events rather than current state; use Kafka topic with infinite retention as event store; derive aggregate state by replaying events; take snapshots every 100 events; support multiple projections and time-travel queries

Implement Saga Orchestration: orchestrator maintains state machine per saga instance; each step emits event upon completion which advances state machine; on step failure trigger compensating actions for all previously completed steps in reverse order; persist saga state durably for crash recovery

Establish consistency guarantees: strong consistency within a service via database transactions; in-order delivery within a partition key; eventual consistency across services (99th percentile convergence under 5 seconds); exactly-once processing via Kafka transactions and consumer idempotency keys

Configure Dead Letter Queue: events failing processing after 3 attempts route to DLQ topic '<domain>.<entity>.<event-type>.dlq'; monitor with dedicated alerting; operations triages daily and replays from DLQ after fixing underlying issue

Set up Confluent Schema Registry: enforce BACKWARD compatibility for all new schema versions; review schema changes as part of PR process; CI pipeline validates compatibility; embed schema IDs in Kafka messages for correct deserialization
rules: Events must represent facts that have already happened (e.g., 'trade.completed') not commands to perform actions (e.g., 'complete.trade')
Events must be self-contained: carry all data consumers need without requiring a callback to the source service
Events are immutable: never modify after publication; publish compensating events for corrections
Events must be versioned: include version number in schema; new fields are optional with defaults; existing fields are never removed or renamed; breaking changes require a new event type
Events must include both producer timestamp (when event occurred) and processing timestamp (when event was ingested)
Partition key must be userId to guarantee ordering per user; cross-key ordering is not guaranteed
Replication factor of 3 for all topics with min ISR of 2 to ensure durability during broker failures
Event retention: 7 days standard, 30 days audit, indefinite for event-sourced aggregates
CQRS command side is the source of truth; query side is eventually consistent and can use different storage technology
Event sourcing snapshots every 100 events to reduce replay time
Saga compensating actions must execute in reverse order of completed steps
Saga state must be persisted durably for crash recovery and resume
Exactly-once processing: use Kafka transactions and consumer idempotency keys
99th percentile cross-service convergence time must be under 5 seconds under normal load
Failed events route to DLQ after 3 processing attempts; DLQ is triaged daily
Schema Registry: BACKWARD compatibility is required; CI validates compatibility; schema IDs embedded in Kafka messages

examples.name: Event Notification Pattern
examples.description: Notification service consumes 'trade.completed' events to send push notifications to both traders, reacting independently with no expectation of a response

examples.name: Event-Carried State Transfer
examples.description: Trading service includes item metadata in 'trade.completed' events so analytics service can generate reports without calling the inventory service API

examples.name: CQRS Implementation
examples.description: Command side uses PostgreSQL for writes with business rule validation; query side consumes events and builds Elasticsearch materialized views for low-latency reads; each side scales independently

examples.name: Event Sourcing with Time Travel
examples.description: Trade aggregate state derived by replaying events from the beginning; snapshots every 100 events for performance; by replaying events up to a specific timestamp, reconstruct any aggregate state at any point in time for debugging and compliance

examples.name: Saga Orchestration - Trade Completion
examples.description: Orchestrator manages: trading-service emits 'trade.settlement.initiated' → inventory-service moves items and emits 'inventory.items.transferred' → economy-service adjusts currencies and emits 'economy.balance.adjusted' → trading-service emits 'trade.settlement.completed'; on failure, compensating events execute in reverse order
